fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out that HTML content processing library we've been talking about. Basically, users are writing raw text and Markdown in our platform and it's showing up broken, unstyled, or sometimes with weird script injections — had a few complaints this week. We need a pipeline that handles all the usual stuff: wrapping plain text safely, rendering Markdown, making URLs clickable, replacing those colon emoji shortcuts with actual images, fixing image paths so they work across environments, proxying external images through our asset service, upgrading HTTP links to HTTPS for our own domain, making images responsive, and handling @mentions so they link to profiles. There's also a need for extracting mentioned usernames from raw Markdown (remember how we did the mention scanning in that social notifications module last quarter — same idea), sanitizing unsafe tags/attributes, syntax highlighting for code blocks, and generating anchor nav links for headings. The emoji image URLs need special handling for names with weird characters — just follow the same URL-encoding approach we've used elsewhere. The proxy feature uses HMAC with the secret to build the URL — the exact algorithm is the same as what the asset team documented internally. Output format details are a bit fuzzy for some features so check the existing test fixtures. Missing config should be reported gracefully rather than just crashing.

trigger_keywords: plain text wrapper output format
what element wraps escaped text
which HTML tag is used for plain text input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Plain text input must be wrapped in a `
` element, HTML-escaped, and the output must end with a trailing newline. For example, input `howdy pahtner` produces `
howdy pahtner
\n`. Characters like `<` and `>` must be escaped to `<` and `>`.

trigger_keywords: soft line breaks behavior
how are newlines handled in Markdown
br tag insertion in paragraphs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Markdown rendering supports a `soft_line_breaks` boolean flag. When `true`, newlines within paragraph text become `
\n` sequences. When `false` or omitted, newlines are preserved as literal newlines inside the paragraph element. List structure is unaffected by this flag.

trigger_keywords: autolink disabled behavior
how to skip URL linking
enabled false for autolink
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The autolink feature accepts an optional `enabled` boolean field. When `enabled` is `false`, the HTML fragment is returned completely unchanged (no URL-to-anchor conversion). When `enabled` is absent or `true`, bare URLs in text nodes are wrapped in `<a href="...">` anchors.

trigger_keywords: emoji image URL format
how is the emoji src constructed
image_asset_path pattern for emoji
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Emoji image `src` is built as `{image_asset_root}/emoji/{emoji_name}.png`. The emoji name portion must be percent-encoded if it contains URL-reserved characters. The image tag format is ``. Missing `image_asset_root` must be reported as `error=missing_required_input`.

trigger_keywords: relative image path resolution
root-relative vs page-relative image src
how are image_base_url and image_page_url used
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Root-relative image paths (starting with `/`) are resolved against `image_base_url`. Page-relative paths (no leading `/`, not already absolute) are resolved against `image_page_url`. Already-absolute URLs are left unchanged. If a required URL context is missing, the adapter must report `error=missing_required_input` and name the missing field.

trigger_keywords: proxy URL construction algorithm
HMAC secret for image proxy
how is the proxied image URL built
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The proxy URL is constructed as `{proxy_endpoint}/{hmac_sha1_hex}/{hex_encoded_original_url}`. The HMAC-SHA1 digest is computed over the original image URL using the `proxy_secret` as the key, output as a lowercase hex string. The original URL is also hex-encoded (each byte as two lowercase hex digits). The original URL is preserved in `data-canonical-src` on the rewritten `` tag.

trigger_keywords: which HTTP links get upgraded to HTTPS
same-site HTTPS upgrade rule
canonical_site_url matching logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only `href` values that exactly match the configured HTTP origin (from `canonical_site_url` or an explicit HTTP site URL field) are upgraded by replacing `http://` with `https://`. Subdomains, different domains, and already-HTTPS links are not modified. If no site URL is provided, report `error=missing_required_input`.

trigger_keywords: responsive image wrapping rules
when is a link wrapper added to images
max-width style on images
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Images without an existing `style` attribute receive `style="max-width:100%;"`. Images not already inside an `<a>` element are wrapped with `<a href="{src}" target="_blank">`. If an image already has a `style` attribute, no style is added and no link wrapper is applied. Images already inside an anchor are not wrapped again.

trigger_keywords: mention link protected tags
which tags are skipped for mention linking
mentions inside pre or code blocks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Mention tokens (`@username`) inside `
`, ``, or existing `` elements must NOT be converted to links — they are left as-is. Valid mentions in normal text become `@username`. The `profile_base_url` field is required.

trigger_keywords: mention collection exclusion rules
what is excluded from extracted mentions
email and domain filtering for mentions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Mention collection from Markdown text must exclude: email addresses (user@domain), domain-like strings, organization/team references (org/team format), and mentions inside inline code. Results are returned in first-seen order without duplicates as `mentions=name1,name2\n`. Hyphenated names and names followed by punctuation are valid and included.

trigger_keywords: which HTML elements are sanitized away
what attributes are stripped by sanitization
unsafe tag removal rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: HTML sanitization removes unsafe elements such as `<script>` and `<style>` entirely (for `<style>`, the inner text content is preserved but the tag is removed). Unsafe attributes like inline `style` are stripped from allowed elements. Invalid table and list structures are also corrected. Allowed elements and attributes follow a whitelist; everything else is stripped while preserving safe inner content.

trigger_keywords: code highlighting failure output
what happens when syntax highlighting fails
processing_failed error format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When code highlighting fails (e.g., due to an unsupported language or a processing error), the output must be exactly `error=processing_failed\ncategory=mentoserror\n`. This applies regardless of whether a `default_language` was supplied or the language was set on the `<pre>` tag.

trigger_keywords: table of contents anchor ID generation
duplicate heading slug handling
heading anchor format for TOC
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each heading element receives an injected anchor: `` inserted after the opening heading tag with a preceding newline. Slugs are lowercased, spaces replaced with hyphens, and special characters removed. Duplicate heading text produces suffixed slugs (`-1`, `-2`, etc.). The `output` field must be `annotated_html` to receive this format.

trigger_keywords: invalid input type error for text features
what error is returned when HTML is passed as text
input type mismatch error code
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Features that expect a `text` string input (plain text wrapping, Markdown rendering) must reject parsed HTML input with `error=invalid_input_type` and an accompanying field indicating the expected input category. The error format follows the standard key=value line format used across all error responses.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the exact HMAC algorithm is the same as what the asset team documented internally

context_pointers.vague_reference: output format details are a bit fuzzy for some features so check the existing test fixtures
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All 13 feature test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` define the exact expected output strings, including trailing newlines, attribute ordering, error key=value line formats, and structural details like the octicon span inside TOC anchors

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a great question but I don't have more detail on that specific point — you'll need to check the existing test fixtures under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or look at how similar behavior was handled in the related features. The test cases are the source of truth for exact output format.

feature10_mention_collection.json.description: Mention tokens are collected from Markdown text as a distinct ordered list while excluding emails, domain-like text, teams, and inline code.

feature11_html_sanitization.json.description: Unsafe HTML elements, attributes, protocols, and invalid table/list structures are removed while preserving allowed content.

feature12_code_highlighting.json.description: Preformatted code blocks are wrapped with language-specific highlighting classes, respecting an existing language over a default.

feature13_table_of_contents.json.description: Heading elements receive stable anchor links and can produce a navigation list with matching href targets.

feature1_plain_text_input.json.description: Plain text is wrapped and HTML-escaped before later HTML processing.

feature2_markdown_rendering.json.description: Markdown text is converted to HTML with configurable GitHub-style line break behavior and fenced-code handling.

Moments matter most

Pointing at the moon

cases.expected_output: Pointing at the moon Reminded of simple things Moments matter most

feature3_autolink.json.description: Bare URLs in HTML text are converted to anchors unless disabled or skipped by tag rules.

feature4_emoji_rendering.json.description: Colon-delimited emoji names are replaced with image tags whose source is built from the configured image asset location.

feature5_image_source_urls.json.description: Relative image sources are rewritten to absolute URLs using either a site image root or the current page URL.

feature6_image_proxying.json.description: External absolute image URLs are rewritten through a configured image proxy while whitelisted hosts and local paths are preserved.

feature7_same_host_https.json.description: Links pointing to the configured HTTP site root are upgraded to HTTPS while subdomains and other domains are left unchanged.

feature8_responsive_images.json.description: Images without existing style are constrained to the container width and linked to their source unless already inside a link.

feature9_mention_links.json.description: User mention tokens in rendered HTML are linked to profile URLs outside protected tags, with safe entity handling.
